questions.question: What three operational gaps separate a memory-only OAuth simulator from production MCP auth?
options: Logging, monitoring, alerting
Client enrollment at scale, JWKS key rotation that breaks boot-time-only fetches, and audience binding enforced on every request
Load balancing, sharding, replication
Password storage, 2FA, captchas
questions.explanation: Production means thousands of clients enrolling without an admin (CIMD/DCR), signing keys that rotate hourly (a boot-time JWKS fetch fails at 3 a.m.), and token.aud checked against the server's canonical URL on every single request.

questions.question: Why did the 2025-11-25 spec make Client ID Metadata Documents (CIMD) the preferred enrollment mechanism over Dynamic Client Registration?
options: CIMD is faster to parse
CIMD inverts push to pull: the client's HTTPS URL IS its client_id, trust roots in DNS, and there's no registration round-trip or per-server state to sync
DCR was found to have a cryptographic flaw
CIMD works without HTTPS
questions.explanation: With CIMD the authorization server fetches the client's metadata from the URL the client controls — no minted client_ids, no namespace exhaustion, no per-server registration. DCR was demoted from SHOULD to MAY but remains the backwards-compatible fallback.

questions.question: Per the spec, what must a client do if the authorization server's metadata lacks code_challenge_methods_supported with S256?
options: Fall back to the implicit flow
Refuse to proceed — absent S256 means no PKCE support, and there is no degraded mode
Use a longer client secret instead
Warn the user but continue
questions.explanation: PKCE is non-negotiable in MCP's OAuth profile. If S256 is missing from the RFC 8414 metadata, the IdP cannot satisfy the contract and the deployment must be rejected — the manifest is wrong, not the code.

questions.question: How does production JWKS handling survive key rotation?
options: Fetch JWKS once at boot and restart the server weekly
A cached key set with a scheduled refresh that overwrites before expiry, plus a fallback fetch on cache miss for tokens signed by newer keys
Disable signature verification during rotation windows
Pin one key forever
questions.explanation: Authorization servers rotate signing keys on schedules (hourly, faster under incident response). The cache-plus-refresh-plus-miss-fallback pattern keeps validation working through rollover without restarts.

questions.question: Which check stops a token issued for one MCP server being replayed against another?
options: Comparing the token's issue time
The resource server comparing token.aud against its own canonical resource URL and rejecting mismatches with 401
Rate limiting per client
TLS alone prevents replay
questions.explanation: Audience pinning is the confused-deputy defense made operational: the RFC 8707 resource indicator becomes a hard aud claim check on every request. Each role owns its checks — the authorization server issues and rotates; the resource server validates.
